title: Low Oil Pressure Shutdown
shortDescription: The engine oil pressure has dropped below the safe operating threshold, triggering an automatic protective shutdown.
fullDescription: Alarm code 1100 indicates that the engine oil pressure sensor has detected pressure below the minimum safe level. Generac home standby and portable generators use a dedicated oil pressure switch or sensor to monitor lubrication at all times. When pressure falls below approximately 7–10 PSI the controller immediately shuts down the engine to prevent catastrophic bearing and cylinder damage. Common root causes include low oil level, a failed oil pressure sensor, blocked oil passages, a worn oil pump, or incorrect oil viscosity for the ambient temperature. This alarm will prevent the generator from restarting until the condition is resolved.

causes.cause: Low or empty engine oil level
Faulty or stuck oil pressure sensor/switch
Worn or failed oil pump producing insufficient pressure
Blocked oil filter or clogged oil passages
Incorrect oil viscosity for operating temperature

symptoms: Generator shuts down unexpectedly and displays alarm 1100
Red LED or alarm light illuminated on control panel
Engine cranks but immediately shuts off after start attempt
Oil level dipstick reads below MIN mark
Generator will not restart after shutdown

fixSteps.title: Check and top off engine oil
fixSteps.instruction: Allow the engine to cool for 5 minutes. Remove the oil dipstick, wipe clean, reinsert fully, and check the level. If below the ADD or MIN mark, add the manufacturer-specified oil (typically 5W-30 synthetic for air-cooled models) until the level reaches FULL. Do not overfill.

fixSteps.title: Inspect for oil leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Examine the area under and around the generator for oil puddles or wet spots. Check the drain plug, oil filter gasket, valve cover gasket, and crankshaft seals. Tighten or replace any leaking components before refilling.

fixSteps.title: Replace the oil and oil filter
fixSteps.instruction: Drain the old oil, replace the oil filter, and refill with fresh oil to the correct level. A clogged filter or degraded oil can cause low pressure readings. Refer to the service manual for the correct oil capacity and filter part number.

fixSteps.title: Test or replace the oil pressure sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the oil pressure switch (typically a single-wire sensor near the oil filter). Disconnect the wire and test sensor resistance with a multimeter — it should show near-zero ohms with no oil pressure and open circuit when pressurized. Replace the sensor if it fails this test or shows erratic readings.

fixSteps.title: Verify oil pump pressure with gauge
fixSteps.instruction: Install a mechanical oil pressure gauge in place of the sensor port. Start the generator briefly and read actual oil pressure. Pressure should be 35–65 PSI at operating RPM. If pressure is low with fresh oil and a good filter, the oil pump or internal bearings require professional inspection.

repairTime: 30 minutes to 2 hours depending on root cause
whenToCallPro: If oil pressure remains low after fresh oil and a new sensor, or if internal engine damage (knocking, scoring) is suspected, contact a Generac-certified technician immediately.
videoSearchQuery: generac generator 1100 low oil pressure alarm fix

faq.q: Can I reset the 1100 alarm without fixing the oil issue?
faq.a: No. The alarm will immediately re-trigger on restart if the underlying low-pressure condition is not corrected. Always resolve the root cause first.

faq.q: What oil type does Generac recommend for home standby generators?
faq.a: Generac recommends 5W-30 full-synthetic oil for most air-cooled models. Check the owner's manual for your specific model, especially for extreme temperature climates.

faq.q: How often should I change the oil on my Generac generator?
faq.a: Generac recommends the first oil change at 20–25 hours of operation, then every 100 hours or annually, whichever comes first. Regular oil changes prevent low-pressure faults.

faq.q: Can a bad oil pressure sensor cause a false 1100 alarm?
faq.a: Yes. A failed or stuck-closed sensor will signal no pressure even when oil level and pump are fine. Test the sensor with a multimeter and replace if faulty.

title: High Engine Temperature Shutdown
shortDescription: The engine temperature has exceeded safe limits and the generator has shut down to prevent engine damage.
fullDescription: Alarm code 1200 is triggered when the engine coolant temperature (liquid-cooled models) or cylinder head temperature (air-cooled models) exceeds the maximum safe threshold. Generac generators use a thermistor or temperature switch to monitor engine heat. When the sensor detects temperatures above approximately 230–250°F (110–121°C), the controller shuts down the engine immediately. Causes include blocked air intake vents, failed cooling fan, low coolant (liquid-cooled), high ambient temperature, running at overload, dirty air filters, or a malfunctioning temperature sensor. Resolving this alarm quickly is critical to preventing permanent engine damage.

causes.cause: Blocked or restricted air intake and exhaust vents on enclosure
Failed or seized cooling fan or shroud damage
Clogged air filter restricting combustion air flow
Low coolant level or coolant leak (liquid-cooled models)
Faulty cylinder head temperature sensor providing false reading

symptoms: Generator shuts down with alarm code 1200 displayed
Engine feels extremely hot to the touch around cylinder head
Burning smell from the generator enclosure
Cooling fan not spinning or operating slowly
Air filter visibly dirty or clogged
Generator shuts down more frequently during hot weather or heavy loads

fixSteps.title: Allow generator to cool and inspect vents
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off and allow the unit to cool for at least 30 minutes. Inspect all enclosure vents and louvers for leaves, debris, snow, or obstructions. Clear any blockages. Ensure the generator has at least 3 feet of clear space on all sides for airflow.

fixSteps.title: Inspect and clean or replace the air filter
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the air filter cover and inspect the filter element. If visibly grey or clogged, replace it. A severely restricted air filter causes the engine to run lean and overheat. Generac recommends annual air filter replacement.

fixSteps.title: Check the cooling fan operation
fixSteps.instruction: With the generator safely off and unplugged from utility, manually spin the cooling fan blade. It should rotate freely. Check for broken blades, debris wrapped around the shaft, and loose mounting bolts. On liquid-cooled models, confirm the electric radiator fan activates when the engine warms up.

fixSteps.title: Check coolant level (liquid-cooled models)
fixSteps.instruction: On Generac Protector and larger liquid-cooled models, locate the coolant overflow reservoir. The level should be between MIN and MAX marks when cold. If low, top off with the correct 50/50 premixed antifreeze. Inspect hoses and the radiator for leaks.

fixSteps.title: Test the temperature sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the cylinder head temperature sensor (air-cooled) or coolant temperature sensor (liquid-cooled). Using a multimeter, measure the sensor resistance and compare against the temperature-resistance chart in the service manual. A failed sensor reading open or short circuit should be replaced.

repairTime: 30 minutes to 3 hours depending on cause
whenToCallPro: If the engine ran hot long enough to cause white smoke, coolant loss, or knocking, stop operation immediately and call a Generac dealer. Internal engine damage requires professional assessment.
videoSearchQuery: generac generator 1200 high engine temperature overheating fix

faq.q: How long should I wait after a 1200 alarm before restarting?
faq.a: Allow at least 30 minutes for the engine to cool completely before attempting a restart. Running a hot engine risks additional damage.

faq.q: Can running my generator in a garage cause a 1200 alarm?
faq.a: Yes. Enclosed spaces restrict airflow and cause heat buildup. Always operate generators outdoors with adequate ventilation, and never inside garages or enclosed structures.

faq.q: Does high ambient temperature cause 1200 alarms?
faq.a: Yes. Generac air-cooled generators are rated to approximately 104°F (40°C) ambient. Operating in higher temperatures or direct sun can trigger 1200 if airflow is also restricted.

faq.q: Can an overloaded generator cause overheating?
faq.a: Yes. Running above rated load causes the engine to work harder and generate excess heat. Shed non-essential loads to keep the generator within its rated wattage.

title: Overcrank — Engine Failed to Start
shortDescription: The engine has failed to start after the maximum number of crank attempts and the controller has shut down.
fullDescription: Alarm code 1300 means the Generac controller attempted to crank the engine for the programmed crank cycle (typically three 10-second attempts with rest intervals) and the engine did not fire and run. The controller then locks out further start attempts to prevent starter motor damage. This alarm is common in cold weather, after extended storage, when the fuel supply is interrupted, or when engine components such as the spark plug, carburetor, or choke are faulty. Standby generators may also trigger 1300 if the LP gas regulator freezes or natural gas pressure is too low during a utility outage.

causes.cause: Low or empty fuel supply (LP, natural gas, or gasoline)
Fouled or failed spark plug(s)
Stuck closed choke or carburetor malfunction
Weak or failed battery not providing sufficient cranking power
Low natural gas or LP pressure at the generator

symptoms: Engine cranks repeatedly but never fires
Generator displays alarm 1300 after crank attempts
Starter motor engages but engine does not catch
No smell of fuel near the exhaust during cranking
Battery voltage indicator reading low before cranking

fixSteps.title: Verify fuel supply
fixSteps.instruction: For LP/natural gas standby units, confirm the manual gas shutoff valve at the generator is fully open. Check LP tank gauge — tank should be at least 20% full. For portable gasoline models, check the fuel level and confirm the fuel shutoff valve is open.

fixSteps.title: Check and replace spark plugs
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the spark plug(s) using a spark plug socket. Inspect for fouling, carbon buildup, cracked ceramic, or burnt electrodes. Replace with the correct NGK or Champion plug specified in your model's manual. Torque to 15 ft-lbs. Gap should be 0.030 inch.

fixSteps.title: Test and charge the battery
fixSteps.instruction: Using a multimeter, check the 12V battery voltage. A fully charged battery reads 12.6–12.8V. If below 12.0V, charge with a standard automotive battery charger. If the battery cannot hold a charge after charging, replace it.

fixSteps.title: Inspect and clean the carburetor (gasoline models)
fixSteps.instruction: Drain old gasoline and remove the carburetor. Spray carburetor cleaner through all jets and passages. Check the choke plate moves freely and the float is not stuck. Reassemble and attempt restart. Severely varnished carburetors should be replaced.

fixSteps.title: Check gas pressure at the generator
fixSteps.instruction: For natural gas and LP standby units, connect a manometer to the test port at the gas valve. Natural gas supply pressure should be 5–7 inches W.C.; LP should be 11 inches W.C. Low pressure indicates an issue with the regulator, supply line, or utility supply. Contact your gas provider if utility supply pressure is low.

whenToCallPro: If the generator still fails to start after replacing spark plugs, charging the battery, and confirming fuel supply, have a technician inspect the ignition module, gas valve, and compression.
videoSearchQuery: generac generator 1300 overcrank failed to start fix

faq.q: How do I reset the 1300 alarm after fixing the issue?
faq.a: Press the OFF button on the controller, wait 30 seconds, then press AUTO. This clears the overcrank lockout and allows the next start attempt.

faq.q: Why does my standby generator get a 1300 alarm in cold weather?
faq.a: Cold temperatures thicken the engine oil and can freeze LP regulators. Ensure the oil is rated for your climate and the LP regulator is properly sized and vaporizing correctly.

faq.q: Can stale gasoline cause a 1300 alarm?
faq.a: Yes. Gasoline over 30 days old can varnish the carburetor and prevent starting. Always use fresh fuel or add a fuel stabilizer for storage periods over 2 weeks.

faq.q: Is the 1300 alarm dangerous to ignore?
faq.a: Repeatedly cranking without diagnosing the cause will drain the battery and wear out the starter motor. Diagnose before attempting more starts.

title: Overspeed Shutdown
shortDescription: The engine exceeded its maximum safe RPM and the controller shut it down to prevent mechanical failure.
fullDescription: Alarm code 1400 is generated when the engine speed exceeds the overspeed threshold — typically more than 10% above rated RPM (63–66 Hz on 60 Hz units). Generac generators are designed to run at 3,600 RPM (60 Hz) or 1,800 RPM (large liquid-cooled units). The controller monitors frequency continuously and shuts the engine down if it over-revs. Causes include a sticking or failed governor, sudden large load drops, a faulty speed sensor, incorrect governor linkage adjustment, or a carburetor running too rich. Overspeed can cause connecting rod failure, valve damage, and other serious mechanical harm if not corrected.

causes.cause: Sticking or failed governor causing uncontrolled throttle opening
Governor linkage bent, disconnected, or improperly adjusted
Carburetor main jet or needle stuck open delivering too much fuel
Sudden complete load shed causing momentary overspeed
Failed or incorrect RPM/speed sensor signal to controller

symptoms: Generator shuts down with alarm 1400 displayed
Engine runs loudly at noticeably higher pitch before shutting down
Frequency meter or display reads above 63 Hz
Generator shuts down immediately after large loads are disconnected
Governor arm or linkage visibly loose or detached

fixSteps.title: Inspect governor linkage and spring
fixSteps.instruction: With the generator off and cool, locate the governor assembly on the engine (usually on the side near the carburetor). Check that the governor arm, control linkage rods, and governor spring are all securely connected, not bent, and move freely without binding. Reconnect or straighten any bent components.

fixSteps.title: Check and clean the carburetor
fixSteps.instruction: A sticking float or needle valve can flood the engine with fuel and cause overspeed. Remove the carburetor, clean with carburetor cleaner, ensure the float moves freely, and check that the needle valve seats properly. Replace any worn parts.

fixSteps.title: Test the governor mechanism
fixSteps.instruction: With the generator running at no load, check that the governor arm moves smoothly when the throttle is manually opened and released. The governor should pull the throttle back as RPM increases. A governor that does not respond indicates an internal failure of the flyweights or governor gear.

fixSteps.title: Check RPM/frequency with a tachometer
fixSteps.instruction: Using a digital tachometer or a clamp meter with frequency function, measure the actual engine RPM and output frequency at no load. It should read 60–61 Hz (3,600–3,660 RPM). If it consistently reads high, adjust the governor per the service manual procedure.

fixSteps.title: Adjust governor speed setting
fixSteps.instruction: Refer to the Generac service manual for your specific engine (OHVI, EH, or iSeries). Governor speed is adjusted by repositioning the governor arm on the governor shaft or adjusting the speed adjusting nut. This must be done at rated no-load speed with a tachometer. Incorrect adjustment can result in further overspeed or underspeed.

whenToCallPro: Governor internal component repair, flyweight replacement, or repeated overspeed events that cause engine noise or vibration require a Generac-certified technician.
videoSearchQuery: generac generator 1400 overspeed governor adjustment fix

faq.q: Can overspeed damage my generator permanently?
faq.a: Yes. Sustained overspeed can bend or break connecting rods, damage valves, and destroy bearings. Address this alarm immediately.

faq.q: Is the 1400 alarm always a governor problem?
faq.a: Not always. A sudden complete load drop (e.g., an air conditioner compressor cycles off) can cause momentary overspeed. If the alarm happens only under this condition, the governor sensitivity may simply need adjustment.

faq.q: How do I know if my governor spring is the right one?
faq.a: Always use Generac OEM springs matched to your specific engine. Aftermarket springs of incorrect tension will cause persistent overspeed or hunting (RPM surging).

faq.q: Can I drive my generator with an overspeed alarm active?
faq.a: No. Operating the generator in an overspeed condition risks catastrophic mechanical failure. Resolve the alarm before using the generator.

title: RPM Sense Loss
shortDescription: The controller has lost the engine speed signal and cannot confirm the engine is running at the correct RPM.
fullDescription: Alarm code 1500 indicates that the Evolution or Nexus controller is not receiving a valid RPM signal from the magnetic pickup sensor or the AC output frequency sensor. The controller uses this signal to confirm successful starting, control fuel delivery, and monitor for overspeed or underspeed conditions. When the signal is absent or invalid, the controller cannot safely operate the generator and will shut down. This fault can occur due to a failed magnetic pickup, broken wiring harness, incorrect sensor gap, a damaged ring gear or flywheel reluctor, or an internal controller fault.

causes.cause: Failed or damaged magnetic pickup sensor
Broken or corroded wiring between sensor and controller
Incorrect air gap between magnetic pickup and ring gear
Damaged ring gear or flywheel missing teeth (no signal produced)
Internal Evolution or Nexus controller failure

symptoms: Generator starts then immediately shuts down with 1500 alarm
Engine runs but controller displays RPM sense loss fault
Generator fails to engage loads after starting
No RPM reading on the digital display during cranking
Intermittent starting that sometimes succeeds and sometimes fails

fixSteps.title: Inspect the magnetic pickup sensor wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the magnetic pickup sensor near the flywheel (typically a threaded cylindrical sensor). Inspect the wiring for cuts, abrasions, corrosion at the connector, and loose terminals. Repair or replace any damaged wiring. Ensure the connector clicks firmly into place.

fixSteps.title: Measure magnetic pickup sensor output
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the sensor connector. Set the multimeter to AC millivolts. Reconnect only the sensor leads and crank the engine. The sensor should produce an AC voltage signal (typically 0.5–5V AC depending on RPM) as the ring gear teeth pass. No signal indicates a failed sensor.

fixSteps.title: Check and set the sensor air gap
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the generator and remove the magnetic pickup sensor. Inspect the tip for metal debris or damage. Reinstall the sensor by threading it in until it bottoms on the ring gear, then back out 1/2 to 3/4 turn. Secure the locknut. The air gap should be 0.020–0.060 inch.

fixSteps.title: Inspect ring gear for missing or damaged teeth
fixSteps.instruction: Using a flashlight, rotate the engine slowly by hand (pull cord or remove spark plug and use a wrench on the crankshaft bolt) while observing the ring gear teeth through the magnetic pickup port. Missing, broken, or severely worn teeth will prevent RPM signal generation.

fixSteps.title: Replace the magnetic pickup sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If the sensor fails the AC output test, replace it with a Generac OEM magnetic pickup. Thread in by hand until snug, back out 1/2 turn, and lock. Reconnect the wiring harness and test for proper operation.

whenToCallPro: If the ring gear is damaged or the Evolution/Nexus controller itself is faulty, professional repair or controller replacement is required. Controller replacement involves programming that requires dealer tools.
videoSearchQuery: generac generator 1500 RPM sense loss magnetic pickup fix

faq.q: What is the difference between alarm 1500 and 1505?
faq.a: Both relate to RPM sense loss. Code 1505 (also listed as E-Code 1505) specifically indicates an RPM sense loss that occurred during the run mode, while 1500 is a general RPM sense loss fault. Diagnosis steps are the same.

faq.q: Can metal debris affect the magnetic pickup sensor?
faq.a: Yes. Metal particles from wear can collect on the sensor tip and interfere with the signal. Clean the sensor tip with a rag whenever you inspect it.

faq.q: Will a 1500 alarm damage the generator if I ignore it?
faq.a: The controller prevents operation without a valid RPM signal, so the generator will not run. It is a protective shutdown, not a destructive one, but the root cause must still be fixed.

faq.q: Can a loose alternator cause a 1500 alarm?
faq.a: On some Generac models that derive the RPM sense from AC output frequency rather than a magnetic pickup, a faulty alternator or bad connection to the controller can trigger 1500. Check the controller connector pins.

title: Undervoltage Fault
shortDescription: The generator output voltage has dropped below the acceptable minimum, indicating an alternator, excitation, or load problem.
fullDescription: Alarm code 1600 is triggered when the generator output voltage falls below approximately 90% of rated voltage (e.g., below 108V on a 120V system or below 198V on a 240V system) for more than a programmed delay. The Evolution controller monitors output voltage on all legs and shuts down the unit to protect connected loads from low-voltage damage. Common causes include a faulty automatic voltage regulator (AVR), a failed capacitor on inverter-type models, a bad excitation winding, overloading of one phase, or a shorted load on the generator output. Temporary voltage sags when starting large motor loads (air conditioners, well pumps) are normal and do not trigger this alarm.

causes.cause: Failed automatic voltage regulator (AVR) no longer maintaining output
Failed or shorted run capacitor on single-phase alternator
Severely overloaded output — loads exceeding generator rated watts
Shorted load or wiring fault on the output circuit
Damaged or open excitation winding in alternator

symptoms: Alarm 1600 displayed and generator shuts down
Lights connected to generator appear dim before shutdown
Voltmeter reading below 108V on 120V circuit
Generator shuts down shortly after connecting large loads
Burning smell from the alternator compartment

fixSteps.title: Check total connected load
fixSteps.instruction: Calculate the total wattage of all loads connected to the generator. Ensure it does not exceed the generator's rated wattage (not surge/peak wattage). Disconnect non-essential loads and test whether the alarm clears.

fixSteps.title: Inspect output connections and load wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the generator and inspect all output terminals, transfer switch connections, and load center wiring for loose terminals, signs of arcing, or shorted circuits. A shorted load will cause voltage collapse. Use a multimeter to check for short circuits on output lines.

fixSteps.title: Test and replace the run capacitor
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the run capacitor in the alternator compartment. Discharge it safely with a resistor (10kΩ, 10W). Test capacitance with a multimeter set to capacitance mode — compare to the rated value printed on the capacitor. Replace if reading is more than 10% below rated or shows open/short.

fixSteps.title: Inspect and test the AVR
fixSteps.instruction: The AVR is typically a small circuit board or module inside the alternator compartment. Check for burnt components, corrosion, or damaged traces. Follow the Generac service manual test procedure using a multimeter to verify AVR output. Replace if faulty.

fixSteps.title: Measure alternator winding resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the alternator output leads. Using a multimeter on ohms, measure the main stator winding resistance across each leg — values should match the spec in the service manual (typically 0.1–0.5 ohms). An open reading indicates a broken winding requiring alternator replacement.

whenToCallPro: AVR replacement, stator testing, and alternator repair require knowledge of high-voltage AC systems. If you are not experienced with generator electrical systems, call a Generac dealer.
videoSearchQuery: generac generator 1600 undervoltage low voltage AVR capacitor fix

faq.q: Can starting a large air conditioner cause a 1600 alarm?
faq.a: A brief voltage sag during motor startup is normal and should not trigger 1600. If it does, the generator may be undersized for the load, or the capacitor/AVR is weakening.

faq.q: Is the run capacitor easy to replace myself?
faq.a: Yes, if you discharge the capacitor safely first. A charged capacitor can deliver a lethal shock. Always discharge before handling.

faq.q: What is a normal output voltage range for a Generac generator?
faq.a: Most Generac 60 Hz generators produce 120/240V ±5%, so roughly 114–126V on each 120V leg. Values consistently below 110V indicate a problem.

faq.q: Can a grounding issue cause a 1600 alarm?
faq.a: An improper neutral-to-ground bond can cause voltage imbalance between legs that might register as undervoltage on one phase. Verify the neutral/ground bonding per the installation manual.

title: Overvoltage Fault
shortDescription: The generator output voltage exceeded the safe upper limit, shutting down to protect connected loads.
fullDescription: Alarm code 1700 is activated when the generator output voltage rises above approximately 110–115% of rated voltage (e.g., above 138V on a 120V circuit) for more than the programmed detection delay. High voltage can destroy appliances, motors, and electronics connected to the generator. The Evolution controller immediately shuts down the engine when this threshold is exceeded. Primary causes include a failed AVR, incorrect AVR adjustment, overspeed (which also raises voltage), a failed sensing circuit, or a shorted AVR component that causes full field excitation. Overvoltage and overspeed often occur together.

causes.cause: Failed AVR applying full field current to alternator
Overspeed condition simultaneously raising voltage (see code 1400)
Incorrect AVR voltage trim adjustment set too high
Failed voltage sensing circuit in controller receiving incorrect feedback
Shorted excitation winding causing uncontrolled field current

symptoms: Alarm 1700 displayed with generator shutdown
Connected appliances or lights fail or act erratically before shutdown
Voltmeter reads above 130V on 120V circuit before shutdown
Overvoltage alarm and overspeed alarm appearing together
Burnt smell from AVR or alternator compartment

fixSteps.title: Verify engine speed is correct
fixSteps.instruction: Using a tachometer or frequency meter, check the output frequency at no load. It must be 60 Hz (3,600 RPM) not significantly higher. If overspeed is present, resolve alarm 1400 first — correcting engine speed will likely also resolve the overvoltage.

fixSteps.title: Check AVR voltage adjustment
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the voltage trimmer potentiometer on the AVR circuit board. With the generator running at no load and a voltmeter on the output, carefully adjust the pot in small increments while monitoring voltage. Target 120–125V. Avoid abrupt adjustments.

fixSteps.title: Test AVR with output load
fixSteps.instruction: Connect a known load (space heater, resistive load bank) equal to roughly 50% of rated watts. The AVR should maintain voltage within ±5% of rated under this load. If voltage continues to climb even under load, the AVR is faulty and should be replaced.

fixSteps.title: Replace the AVR
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect battery and output leads. Remove the alternator end cover. Disconnect AVR wiring (document connections with a photo first). Install the new OEM AVR, reconnect wiring per the diagram, and retest output voltage at no load and 50% load.

whenToCallPro: If overvoltage persists after AVR replacement, or if excitation windings are damaged, a Generac dealer with proper test equipment must service the alternator.
videoSearchQuery: generac generator 1700 overvoltage AVR replacement fix

faq.q: Will a 1700 alarm damage my appliances?
faq.a: If the alarm fires quickly enough the shutdown protects loads. However if overvoltage persists before the controller reacts, sensitive electronics can be damaged. Check all connected equipment after a 1700 event.

faq.q: Is code 1700 always an AVR issue?
faq.a: Not always. Overspeed is the other major cause because voltage is proportional to RPM in a synchronous alternator. Always check engine speed first.

faq.q: Can I adjust the AVR myself?
faq.a: Yes, with care. Make only small adjustments and monitor voltage with a calibrated meter. Adjusting too high re-creates the overvoltage condition; too low triggers code 1600.

faq.q: How long does a Generac AVR typically last?
faq.a: Under normal conditions, AVRs last 10–20 years. Failure is accelerated by moisture intrusion, heat, and voltage spikes from loads switching in and out.

title: Internal Fault — Controller Error
shortDescription: The Evolution or Nexus controller has detected an internal error and has shut down the generator.
fullDescription: Alarm code 1800 is a generic controller-detected internal fault. Unlike specific sensor-triggered alarms, 1800 indicates that the Evolution or Nexus controller board itself has identified an internal inconsistency, memory error, program execution fault, or communication failure between internal subsystems. This alarm can also appear after lightning-induced voltage surges, power supply issues on the 12V control bus, or firmware corruption. In some cases, 1800 is a secondary alarm that follows another primary alarm that caused the controller to enter a faulted state. Thorough diagnosis involves clearing the alarm, reviewing the event log, and testing the control board power supply before committing to controller replacement.

causes.cause: Transient voltage surge or lightning strike corrupting controller memory
Low or unstable 12V battery voltage causing controller brownout
Corroded or loose connector on the controller board
Failed Evolution or Nexus controller board
Firmware corruption requiring reflash by dealer

symptoms: Alarm 1800 displayed with no obvious mechanical cause
Controller display freezes, blanks, or shows garbled characters
Generator fails to enter AUTO mode after alarm clear
Multiple different alarms appearing in sequence
Generator ran normally then suddenly faulted with no external trigger

fixSteps.title: Perform a full controller power cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Set the controller to OFF. Disconnect the 12V battery negative cable and wait 5 minutes. Reconnect the battery, set to AUTO, and observe whether the alarm reappears. A power cycle clears transient memory errors.

fixSteps.title: Check the 12V battery voltage and charging
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the battery voltage at rest (should be 12.6V or higher) and during cranking (should not drop below 9.6V). Also measure battery charger output — the controller maintains the battery at 13.5–13.8V during standby. Weak battery or failed charger can cause 1800 faults.

fixSteps.title: Inspect controller connector pins
fixSteps.instruction: With the generator off and battery disconnected, unplug the main wiring harness connectors from the controller board. Inspect all pins for corrosion, bent pins, or pushed-back terminals. Clean with electrical contact cleaner and a pin cleaning tool. Reconnect firmly.

fixSteps.title: Review the event log
fixSteps.instruction: Access the controller event log via the menu (Evolution: Menu > History > Event Log; Nexus: button sequence per manual). Review the last several entries to identify if a primary alarm preceded the 1800. Resolving the primary alarm may resolve 1800.

fixSteps.title: Contact Generac dealer for controller replacement or reflash
fixSteps.instruction: If the alarm persists after power cycling, fresh battery, and connector inspection, the controller board likely requires replacement or firmware reflash. These operations require Generac dealer tools and are not user-serviceable. Document all alarm codes before calling.

whenToCallPro: Controller board replacement and firmware flashing require Generac dealer tools and training. Do not attempt to flash firmware without the proper Generac service interface.
videoSearchQuery: generac generator 1800 internal controller fault fix reset

faq.q: Can lightning cause a 1800 fault?
faq.a: Yes. Nearby lightning strikes can induce voltage spikes through the generator output, antenna wiring, or ground system that corrupt the controller. Surge protectors and a proper ground rod help minimize risk.

faq.q: Will resetting the controller erase my alarm history?
faq.a: A battery disconnection resets the controller state but the Evolution controller retains its event log in non-volatile memory. Your alarm history will still be accessible after reconnecting power.

faq.q: Is the Evolution controller interchangeable between generator models?
faq.a: No. Each controller is programmed specifically for the engine and alternator combination. Always use the OEM part number for your exact model.

faq.q: Can I replace the controller myself?
faq.a: Physically replacing the board is straightforward, but the new controller must be programmed with the correct model parameters using Generac dealer software. Plan on dealer programming after board swap.

title: Stepper Motor Overcurrent Fault
shortDescription: The stepper motor that controls the fuel throttle has drawn excessive current, indicating a mechanical bind or motor failure.
fullDescription: Alarm code 1900 indicates that the Evolution controller detected an overcurrent condition in the stepper motor driver circuit. The stepper motor on Generac standby generators controls the throttle position (fuel mixture on carbureted models, throttle on EFI models) and operates continuously while the generator runs to maintain stable RPM under varying loads. When the motor draws more current than the driver can safely supply — due to a bound throttle shaft, damaged gearbox, shorted motor winding, or a failed stepper driver on the controller — the controller shuts down to prevent driver IC damage. Code 1900 is most common on Guardian and PowerPact series equipped with the Evolution controller.

causes.cause: Throttle shaft or choke binding from corrosion or debris
Faulty stepper motor with shorted or open winding
Damaged stepper motor gearbox or stripped gear
Failed stepper motor driver circuit on the Evolution controller
Corroded or damaged wiring harness to stepper motor

symptoms: Alarm 1900 displayed and generator shuts down
Generator surges (hunting) in RPM before the 1900 alarm appears
Stepper motor connector warm to the touch
Throttle shaft feels stiff or does not spring back freely
Generator worked normally then suddenly faulted with alarm 1900

fixSteps.title: Inspect throttle and stepper linkage for binding
fixSteps.instruction: With the generator off, locate the stepper motor (small servo-like motor attached to the throttle body). Manually move the throttle shaft through its full range of motion. It should move smoothly with light spring return. Corrosion or debris causing binding will overload the motor.

fixSteps.title: Disconnect and test the stepper motor windings
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the stepper motor connector. Using a multimeter, measure resistance across each pair of stepper motor winding terminals. A typical 4-wire bipolar stepper should show equal resistance (usually 5–50 ohms) across each coil pair. Open or shorted windings indicate motor failure.

fixSteps.title: Clean and lubricate the throttle assembly
fixSteps.instruction: Apply a light spray of electrical contact cleaner to the throttle shaft bushings. Work the throttle back and forth to free any corrosion. Once clean and moving freely, apply a small amount of white lithium grease to the shaft. Do not spray cleaner into the carburetor venturi.

fixSteps.title: Replace the stepper motor
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the motor connector and remove the 2–4 mounting screws securing the stepper motor to the throttle body. Install the replacement OEM stepper motor, being careful to align the drive gear or arm correctly. Reconnect wiring. Power cycle and test.

whenToCallPro: If alarm 1900 returns immediately after stepper motor replacement, the stepper driver circuitry on the Evolution controller board is failed and requires dealer service.
videoSearchQuery: generac generator 1900 stepper overcurrent motor replacement fix

faq.q: Is the stepper motor replaceable without replacing the entire throttle body?
faq.a: On most Guardian models the stepper motor is a separate component that bolts onto the throttle body, so it can be replaced independently. Always confirm with your parts diagram.

faq.q: Can the 1900 alarm clear itself?
faq.a: No. A 1900 fault requires a manual reset (OFF button, then AUTO). The binding or motor failure must be repaired before the reset will hold.

faq.q: Does cold weather cause stepper overcurrent?
faq.a: Yes. In very cold weather, grease in the throttle shaft can thicken significantly, increasing resistance and causing the stepper to draw excess current. Annual lubrication with appropriate grease helps prevent this.

faq.q: How do I identify the stepper motor on my Generac?
faq.a: The stepper motor is a small rectangular or cylindrical motor with a multi-pin connector, mounted directly on or adjacent to the carburetor throttle body. It typically has 4 or 6 wires.

title: Overload — Remove Load Shutdown
shortDescription: The generator is being asked to supply more power than its rated capacity and has shut down to prevent damage.
fullDescription: Alarm code 2000 indicates that the generator output current has exceeded the rated load capacity for long enough to trigger the overload protection. Generac generators are rated for continuous output at their nameplate wattage — typically measured in kW at unity power factor. Motor loads such as air conditioners, well pumps, and refrigerators draw 3–6 times their running wattage during startup, which can momentarily push the generator into overload territory. If the total running load itself exceeds the generator rating, alarm 2000 will appear and the unit will shut down. On portable generators, this alarm may also come from plugging in more extension cords than the unit can supply.

causes.cause: Total connected loads exceeding the generator's rated continuous output
Large motor (AC, well pump) inrush current exceeding surge rating
Shorted or failed load wiring creating abnormally high current draw
Faulty circuit breaker not tripping on its own, leading to overload alarm
Failed output current sensor providing false overload reading

symptoms: Alarm 2000 displayed and generator shuts down
Engine bogs down or slows before the shutdown occurs
Circuit breaker on generator trips (portable models)
Some loads connected but power suddenly lost to all
Generator shuts down when specific high-wattage appliance is turned on

fixSteps.title: Disconnect all loads and reset
fixSteps.instruction: Turn all loads off or disconnect them from the generator. Press OFF on the controller, then AUTO (standby) or START (portable). Once the generator is running cleanly at no load, reconnect loads one at a time starting with the highest priority, monitoring voltage and engine sound.

fixSteps.title: Calculate total load wattage
fixSteps.instruction: Add up the running watts of all loads you intend to power simultaneously. Do not forget to account for motor starting surge (typically 3× the running watts). The total must be below the generator's rated kW output. If over-rated, shed non-essential loads permanently.

fixSteps.title: Stagger motor load starts
fixSteps.instruction: Start large motor loads (AC, well pump, refrigerator) one at a time with a 30-second gap between each. This prevents multiple inrush currents from overlapping and exceeding the generator's surge rating.

fixSteps.title: Inspect circuit breakers
fixSteps.instruction: Check all generator output breakers and the transfer switch breakers. A breaker that is tripped to the middle position is not providing power. Reset any tripped breakers after removing the overloading load. Replace any breakers that no longer hold.

fixSteps.title: Inspect for shorted load wiring
fixSteps.instruction: If the alarm appears even with minimal loads, disconnect all loads and use a multimeter to check for short circuits on the output wiring. A shorted neutral or phase wire will cause constant overload readings. Inspect for damaged insulation, especially in areas subject to rodent activity.

whenToCallPro: If the overload alarm appears with no loads connected, or if output wiring inspection reveals damaged wiring in the transfer switch or load center, a licensed electrician or Generac dealer should inspect the system.
videoSearchQuery: generac generator 2000 overload remove load fix reset

faq.q: How do I know what size generator I need to avoid code 2000?
faq.a: Calculate the running watt total of all loads you want to power, then add the highest single motor's starting surge. The result is the minimum generator kW you need. Generac's generator size calculator tool can assist with this.

faq.q: Can a 2000 alarm damage the generator?
faq.a: The controller shuts down before damage occurs in most cases. However, repeated overload events accelerate wear on the alternator windings and circuit breakers.

faq.q: Why does my 22kW Guardian trip 2000 when the central air comes on?
faq.a: A large 5-ton central AC can draw 15–20 kW of starting surge. If other loads are already running, the combined demand may briefly exceed 22 kW. Stage your loads or check if the AC has a soft-starter installed.

faq.q: Does code 2000 mean my generator is broken?
faq.a: Not necessarily. If the cause is a legitimate overload, the generator is working correctly by protecting itself. Reduce the load and the alarm will clear.

title: Wiring Harness Fault
shortDescription: The controller has detected an open circuit, short circuit, or incorrect signal in the main engine wiring harness.
fullDescription: Alarm code 1511 is issued by the Evolution or Nexus controller when it detects a wiring harness integrity problem — specifically an open or short circuit condition in the control harness that connects the controller to the engine sensors, solenoids, and actuators. The controller continuously performs self-checks on connected circuits; when a resistance is out of range for any harness wire, 1511 is logged. Common causes include rodent damage (a well-known issue in Generac and other outdoor generator brands), heat-cracked insulation, corroded multi-pin connectors, chafed wiring against moving engine parts, or water intrusion into connectors after rain or pressure washing.

causes.cause: Rodent damage — chewed insulation on control harness wires
Corroded or water-intruded multi-pin connector on the main harness
Chafed wiring where harness contacts engine block, exhaust, or moving parts
Heat-cracked insulation on wires near the exhaust manifold
Factory wiring defect or improper harness routing at installation

symptoms: Alarm 1511 displayed, generator shuts down or fails to start
Visible chewed wiring or nesting material inside the generator enclosure
Other intermittent sensor alarms appearing alongside 1511
Alarm worsens after rain or washing the generator area
Burning or melted plastic smell inside the generator enclosure

fixSteps.title: Inspect the harness for rodent damage or chafing
fixSteps.instruction: Open the generator enclosure and visually inspect the entire control wiring harness from the controller to each sensor and solenoid. Look for chewed insulation, bare copper, nesting material, or mouse droppings. Mark any damaged areas with tape before proceeding to repair.

fixSteps.title: Inspect and clean all harness connectors
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect each multi-pin connector in the harness. Inspect for corroded, bent, or pushed-back pins. Clean with electrical contact cleaner and a small brush. Apply dielectric grease to all connector faces before reconnecting.

fixSteps.title: Repair damaged wiring
fixSteps.instruction: For chewed or damaged wires, use Posi-Tap connectors or soldered splice repairs covered with heat-shrink tubing. Electrical tape alone is not adequate for outdoor/engine environments. Route repaired wires away from heat sources and secure with cable ties.

fixSteps.title: Test harness continuity with a multimeter
fixSteps.instruction: After repairs, with the generator off and battery disconnected, use a multimeter in continuity mode to test each harness wire from the controller connector to its sensor or load. Any open circuit reading indicates a break still remaining. Resistance between any harness wire and ground should be very high (open) unless it is a ground wire.

fixSteps.title: Replace the wiring harness if damage is extensive
fixSteps.instruction: If damage covers multiple wires or the harness is severely degraded, order the OEM replacement harness for your model. Label or photograph all connections before removing the old harness. Install the new harness following the same routing, securing all clamps and cable ties.

repairTime: 1 to 4 hours depending on damage extent
whenToCallPro: Extensive harness damage, harness routing into the engine block area, or persistent 1511 after harness repair or replacement should be evaluated by a Generac dealer.
videoSearchQuery: generac generator 1511 wiring harness fault rodent damage repair

faq.q: Why do rodents target generator wiring harnesses?
faq.a: Generators provide a warm, dark, dry shelter that attracts rodents, especially in winter. The soy-based insulation used on modern automotive-style wiring is also attractive to rodents as a food source.

faq.q: How can I prevent rodent damage in my Generac enclosure?
faq.a: Wrap harnesses with capsaicin-infused rodent-repellent tape (Honda and 3M make products), place rodent deterrent pouches inside the enclosure, and seal any openings at the base of the enclosure with steel wool and foam.

faq.q: Can I use electrical tape alone to repair chewed wires?
faq.a: No. Electrical tape degrades quickly in the heat and moisture of an engine compartment. Always use soldered splices with adhesive-lined heat-shrink tubing for durable repairs.

faq.q: Will insurance cover rodent damage to my Generac?
faq.a: Some homeowners' insurance policies cover generator damage under certain circumstances, but rodent damage is often excluded as a maintenance issue. Check your specific policy and contact Generac for a damage assessment report.

title: Service A and Service B Maintenance Schedule
shortDescription: The generator has reached its scheduled maintenance interval and requires Service A or Service B preventive maintenance.
fullDescription: Generac home standby generators equipped with the Evolution controller display a 'Service A' or 'Service B' notification when the engine has accumulated the programmed number of operating hours (typically every 200 hours or annually, whichever comes first). Service A is the lighter interval and includes oil and filter change, air filter inspection, spark plug check, and battery test. Service B is a more comprehensive service adding spark plug replacement, fuel filter replacement (where applicable), valve clearance check, and transfer switch inspection. These are not error codes — they are scheduled maintenance reminders designed to ensure the generator remains reliable for emergency use. On the Evolution controller, the notification appears at startup and in the event log.

causes.cause: Engine has reached 200 hours of operation or 12 months since last service
Generator recently installed but maintenance counter not reset after initial dealer setup
Service performed but maintenance counter not reset in the controller menu
Controller maintenance interval programmed shorter than default by previous service

symptoms: Controller displays 'Service A' or 'Service B' at startup
Event log contains a scheduled maintenance entry
Yellow service indicator light illuminated on Evolution panel
Generator continues to function normally despite the notification
Service reminder reappears after each startup until reset

fixSteps.title: Perform Service A — oil and filter change
fixSteps.instruction: Warm up the generator for 5 minutes, then shut it down. Drain the engine oil using the drain valve or plug. Replace the oil filter. Refill with the correct amount and type of oil (check owner's manual — typically 1.7–2 quarts of 5W-30 full synthetic). Check the air filter and replace if dirty. Test the 12V battery voltage.

fixSteps.title: Perform Service B additional tasks
fixSteps.instruction: In addition to Service A tasks: replace the spark plug(s) with the OEM-specified plug gapped to 0.030 inch. Replace the fuel filter on LP/natural gas models (inline sediment filter if equipped). Check valve clearance on OHVI engines per the service manual specification (intake: 0.004–0.006 inch, exhaust: 0.006–0.008 inch).

fixSteps.title: Inspect transfer switch and connections
fixSteps.instruction: With the utility power on and the generator in AUTO, inspect the transfer switch for signs of arcing, loose connections, or corrosion on the bus bars and contactors. Tighten any loose terminal screws to the specified torque. Replace any visibly damaged contactors.

fixSteps.title: Reset the maintenance counter on the Evolution controller
fixSteps.instruction: On Evolution controllers: press and hold the ENTER button for 3 seconds to enter the service menu. Navigate to 'Reset Maintenance' or 'Service Reset' using the arrow buttons, then confirm. The service notification will clear immediately. Nexus controllers: refer to the specific model's owner's manual for the reset sequence.

fixSteps.title: Run the generator under load and verify
fixSteps.instruction: Manually transfer to generator power (or disconnect utility briefly in test mode) and run the generator under actual home load for 10–15 minutes. Confirm steady output voltage (120/240V ±5%), steady frequency (60 Hz), no new alarms, and no unusual sounds or smells. Log the service in the maintenance record.

repairTime: 1 to 3 hours for complete Service A or B
whenToCallPro: If valve clearance is out of spec, transfer switch contactors are pitted or damaged, or you are not comfortable performing service tasks, schedule with a Generac-authorized dealer. Annual dealer service is recommended for generators under warranty.
videoSearchQuery: generac generator service A B maintenance oil change reset

faq.q: What happens if I ignore the Service A or B reminder?
faq.a: The generator will continue to operate, but degraded oil, a dirty air filter, and worn spark plugs reduce reliability and can eventually cause mechanical failures like low oil pressure (1100) or overcrank (1300) faults.

faq.q: How do I know if I need Service A or Service B?
faq.a: Service A is required every 200 hours or annually. Service B is required every 400 hours or every two years. The Evolution controller event log will specify which service interval has been triggered.

faq.q: Can I use any brand of 5W-30 oil in my Generac?
faq.a: Generac approves any API-certified 5W-30 full-synthetic engine oil. Generac-branded oil is convenient but not required. Avoid oils with high friction modifiers marketed for fuel economy.

faq.q: My generator only runs during power outages which are rare — does it still need annual service?
faq.a: Yes. Even if the generator runs very few hours, oil degrades with age and the weekly exercise cycle (typically 12 minutes) accumulates operating time. Annual service is still required regardless of run hours.

faq.q: How do I log service history in the Evolution controller?
faq.a: The Evolution controller does not store service records; it only tracks operating hours and logs alarm events. Keep a written or digital service log with the date, hours, and tasks performed for warranty and resale purposes.
